title: Before the Last Snowfall
synopsis: Otto the baby otter discovers his friend Mabel is planning to leave their mountain cabin home for distant places. Through three days of adventure, invention, and quiet understanding, Otto learns that friendship can stretch across any distance, even to the stars.

chapters.title: The Sound of Packing
chapters.content:
The attic floorboards creaked in a pattern I'd never heard before.

I pressed my tiny paws against the warm stones around our fireplace and listened. Mabel's room was directly above me, and she usually walked with soft, careful steps. But this morning her feet moved in short bursts, then stopped, then moved again. Like she was picking things up and putting them down.

The fire crackled and sent orange sparks up the chimney. Our cabin always smelled like pine needles and hot cocoa, but today there was something else. Something that reminded me of the time Mabel showed me her collection of maps with strange symbols and dotted lines leading to places with no names.

I climbed the narrow stairs, my whiskers twitching. Mabel's door was open just a crack, and I could see the corner of her wooden trunk. It was the one she kept under her bed, the one that was supposed to be empty.

It wasn't empty anymore.

Inside were silver instruments that looked like they belonged in the sky, rolled papers tied with red ribbons, and a thick coat with more pockets than anyone could possibly need. Everything was folded with the kind of care you use when you're planning to be away for a very long time.

Mabel didn't notice me watching. She was too busy wrapping something round and glowing in soft cloth. When she held it up to the window, it cast tiny points of light across the ceiling like indoor stars.

"Otto?" she called suddenly. "Would you like to help me with something?"

I pretended I'd just arrived at her door. "What kind of something?"

"The kind that requires an excellent assistant with small paws and a talent for finding lost things."

This was obviously true, so I padded into her room and sat down next to the trunk. Up close, I could see that one of the silver instruments had tiny dials and a needle that pointed not north, but up.

"Where does that one point?" I asked.

Mabel looked at the compass in my paws, then out the window at the falling snow. "Somewhere I've been planning to visit for a long time."

"How long?"

"Since before you moved into the cabin with me. Since before the first snow. Since... well, since I learned that some journeys can't wait forever."

She sat down beside me and opened one of the rolled papers. It was covered in dotted trails and compass roses, but instead of leading to mountains or rivers, this map showed the space between stars.

"The weather window opens in three days," Mabel said quietly. "If I miss it, I'll have to wait another whole year."

I studied the map more carefully. The dotted trail started at our mountain and curved up, up, up until it reached a red planet with two tiny moons.

"That's quite far," I observed.

"Yes," Mabel agreed. "It is."

illustrations.description: A chubby baby otter with soft chestnut-brown fur, sparkling dark eyes, tiny paws, and floppy whiskers peers through a slightly open door at a wooden trunk filled with silver instruments and mysterious maps. The room has a cozy mountain cabin feel with snow visible through the window. Treasure map style illustration with aged parchment backgrounds, sepia tones, and jewel accents on the mysterious instruments.
illustrations.alt: Otto watching Mabel pack strange instruments and maps

chapters.title: The Invention Workshop
chapters.content:
The next morning, I woke up with a brilliant idea.

If Mabel was planning to travel to distant planets, she would need proper equipment. And since I was an excellent inventor of useful things, I could build her something that would make the journey easier.

I gathered my supplies from around the cabin: empty soup cans, rubber bands, the magnifying glass from Mabel's desk, and a collection of springs I'd been saving for exactly this sort of emergency.

In the workshop area I'd set up near the fireplace, I began constructing what I was certain would be the most helpful travel device ever created. It had three extending arms for reaching things, a small mirror for looking around corners, and a bell that rang whenever it detected something interesting.

The fire warmed my fur as I worked, and snow continued to fall outside the tall windows. Our cabin felt extra cozy when the weather was like this, which made it even more difficult to understand why anyone would want to leave.

Mabel came downstairs while I was attaching the final spring.

"What are you building?" she asked, settling into her favorite chair with a cup of steaming tea.

"A Universal Adventure Assistant," I explained, testing the extending arms. "It's for people who need to explore places that are very far away and possibly filled with unknown challenges."

"I see. And what does it do?"

I demonstrated each feature. The arms could stretch to reach high shelves or pick up dropped items. The mirror could help you see around corners or behind you. The bell would ring if it detected anything worth investigating.

"It's very thoughtful," Mabel said. "But Otto, you know that where I'm going, I'll have equipment provided by the Space Research Division."

This was news to me. "You're going on official business?"

"I've been selected for the advance settlement team. We're going to Mars to set up the first permanent research station." Mabel's eyes sparkled the same way they did when she showed me new constellations. "I'll be studying the soil to see what kinds of plants might grow there."

I looked down at my invention, then back at Mabel. "How long will you be gone?"

"The mission is scheduled for two years. But Otto, that's not very long in the grand scheme of things."

Two years sounded like a very long time to me. Two years was longer than I'd known Mabel. Two years was longer than I'd lived in the cabin.

"Will you write letters?" I asked.

"Every week," Mabel promised. "And I'll send pictures of what Mars looks like up close. Did you know it has storms that can cover the entire planet?"

I didn't know that, but it didn't sound like the sort of place that would have cozy mountain cabins with fireplaces.

"Maybe you could postpone the trip," I suggested. "Just until the weather improves."

Mabel smiled, but it was the kind of smile that meant she'd already made up her mind about something. "The weather won't improve for Mars, Otto. This is the kind of adventure that doesn't wait."

illustrations.description: A chubby baby otter with soft chestnut-brown fur, sparkling dark eyes, tiny paws, and floppy whiskers works intently on a contraption made of soup cans, springs, and various mechanical parts near a crackling fireplace. Snow falls outside the windows of the cozy mountain cabin. The invention has extending arms and a small bell. Treasure map style with aged parchment feel, sepia tones, and jewel accents on the mechanical parts.
illustrations.alt: Otto building his Universal Adventure Assistant by the fireplace

chapters.title: The Problem with Distance
chapters.content:
On the second day, I discovered the real problem with Mabel's plan.

I'd been studying her Mars maps while she was outside checking the weather instruments, and I'd calculated the distance using my whiskers as measuring tools. The journey from our mountain cabin to Mars was approximately forty-seven million whisker-lengths.

This seemed like too far for weekly letters.

When Mabel came back inside, stamping snow off her boots, I presented my findings.

"According to my measurements," I announced, "Mars is too far away for proper friendship maintenance."

Mabel hung her coat by the fire and sat down at the kitchen table. "What do you mean by friendship maintenance?"

"Well," I explained, "friendships require regular attention. Like plants or fires or sourdough starters. You have to tend them, or they get weak and eventually go out."

This was obviously true, but Mabel looked puzzled. "Otto, do you think our friendship will go out while I'm away?"

I considered this carefully. "It might. Forty-seven million whisker-lengths is a significant distance. And space doesn't have reliable postal service."

"Actually," Mabel said, "space has very reliable communication systems. I'll be able to send messages that travel at the speed of light."

"How fast is that?"

"Fast enough that a message from Mars would reach you in about twenty minutes."

I was impressed despite myself. "That is quite fast. But what about the other friendship maintenance activities? Like building snow forts together, or sharing hot cocoa, or working on inventions?"

Mabel was quiet for a moment. Outside, the wind picked up and rattled the cabin windows. "Those things are important," she agreed. "But Otto, there are also other ways to share adventures. When I send you pictures of Martian sunsets, you'll see things that no earth-bound creature has ever seen before. When I tell you about the first seeds sprouting in Martian soil, you'll be part of a discovery that changes everything."

"But I'll be here, and you'll be there."

"Yes," Mabel said. "But friendship isn't just about being in the same place. It's about caring what happens to each other, no matter where we are."

I wanted to argue with this, but I wasn't sure how. It sounded like the kind of thing that might be true even if I didn't like it.

"What if something goes wrong?" I asked. "What if your rocket gets lost, or Mars turns out to be unfriendly, or you forget about the cabin?"

"Then I'll figure it out," Mabel said simply. "The same way I figured out how to build weather instruments, and how to read star charts, and how to take care of baby otters who show up at mountain cabins during snowstorms."

I didn't remember showing up during a snowstorm, but Mabel sometimes remembered things differently than I did.

illustrations.description: A chubby baby otter with soft chestnut-brown fur, sparkling dark eyes, tiny paws, and floppy whiskers sits at a wooden table covered with Mars maps, using his whiskers to measure distances on the star charts. A cozy mountain cabin surrounds him with warm firelight, and snow continues to fall outside. Treasure map style illustration with aged parchment backgrounds, compass roses on the maps, sepia tones, and jewel accents on the measuring instruments.
illustrations.alt: Otto measuring distances on Mars maps with his whiskers

chapters.title: The Weather Window
chapters.content:
The third day arrived with clear skies and bitter cold.

Mabel spent the morning making final preparations. She checked her instruments, rolled her maps one last time, and packed the glowing compass that pointed up instead of north. The transport vehicle would arrive that afternoon to take her to the launch facility.

I sat by the window and watched her work. Everything she did had a sense of finality to it, like she was saying goodbye to the cabin in small, careful steps.

"Otto," she said eventually, "I have something for you."

She handed me a small device that looked like a simplified version of her space communicator. It had a single button and a tiny screen that currently showed nothing but stars.

"This is connected to my mission communications array," Mabel explained. "Whenever you press the button, it will show you exactly what I'm looking at. And if you hold it down for three seconds, it will send me a signal letting me know you're thinking about me."

"What kind of signal?"

"A small light that appears on my equipment. Like a friendly firefly, even in space."

I tested the button. The screen flickered and showed me a view of our cabin from outside, which meant Mabel was looking at me through the window.

"It works very well," I observed.

"I thought you might like having a way to check on my adventures."

We heard the rumble of engines before we saw the transport vehicle. It was larger than I'd expected, with thick treads for navigating mountain snow and a compartment specifically designed for carrying space mission equipment.

Mabel gathered her belongings and carried them outside. I followed, even though the cold made my whiskers sting.

The transport driver helped load Mabel's trunk and equipment cases. Everything fit perfectly, like this moment had been planned for months.

"Ready?" the driver asked.

Mabel looked back at the cabin, then down at me. "Otto, I want you to know that this cabin will always be our place. When I come back, we'll sit by the fire and I'll tell you everything about Mars that you can't see through the communicator."

"Will you tell me about the planet-wide storms?"

"Every detail."

"And the soil experiments?"

"All of them."

"And whether Mars has good places for building snow forts?"

Mabel laughed. "Mars doesn't have snow, Otto. It has dust storms and frozen carbon dioxide. But I'll tell you about those too."

She climbed into the transport vehicle and rolled down the window. "Take care of the cabin. And don't forget to keep the fire going."

"I won't forget," I promised.

The transport vehicle rumbled to life and began making its way down the mountain road. I watched until it disappeared around a bend, then looked down at the communicator in my paws.

The screen showed the inside of the transport vehicle, and Mabel looking out the window at the same mountain scenery I was watching. Even though she was already far away, we were seeing the same things at the same time.

I pressed and held the button for three seconds, sending a firefly signal across whatever distance separated us.

illustrations.description: A chubby baby otter with soft chestnut-brown fur, sparkling dark eyes, tiny paws, and floppy whiskers stands in deep snow outside a mountain cabin, watching a large transport vehicle disappear around a snowy mountain bend. In his tiny paws he holds a small communication device showing a glowing screen. The scene has clear winter skies and evergreen trees. Treasure map style illustration with aged parchment feel, sepia tones, and jewel accents on the communication device.
illustrations.alt: Otto watching Mabel's transport vehicle leave while holding the communicator

chapters.title: Messages from Mars
chapters.content:
Six months later, I received my first picture from Mars.

It arrived on the communicator just as I was testing a new invention—a device for roasting marshmallows at exactly the right distance from the fire. The screen lit up and showed me something I'd never seen before: a sunrise over alien mountains, painted in colors that Earth didn't seem to have.

Below the image, text appeared in Mabel's familiar handwriting: "Otto, the sunrises here last twice as long as they do at the cabin. I think you'd appreciate having extra time to watch the light change."

She was right. I did appreciate having extra time for that sort of thing.

Over the following weeks, more pictures arrived. Mars dust devils that moved like dancers across red plains. Rock formations that looked like sleeping giants. The first tiny green shoots emerging from carefully tended soil in the research station greenhouse.

Each picture came with a message, and each message felt like Mabel was sitting across from me at the kitchen table, sharing stories about her day.

"The dust gets into everything," one message read, "but it makes the most remarkable sunsets. They're purple and green and gold all at once."

"We discovered water ice just below the surface near the north habitat," said another. "It tastes exactly like the snowmelt from our mountain stream."

I started sending firefly signals more frequently. Three seconds for "I'm thinking about you." Five seconds for "Something interesting happened today." Seven seconds for "I wish you could see what I'm seeing."

Mabel began interpreting my signals correctly, which proved that friendship maintenance worked just as well across space as it did across a room.

One evening, as snow fell outside the cabin windows, I pressed the communicator button to check on Mabel's current view. The screen showed the inside of the Mars research station, and Mabel working at a laboratory table covered with soil samples and tiny plants.

But what caught my attention was the wall behind her. She'd hung up pictures of our mountain cabin, the fireplace, and the view from our kitchen window during last winter's biggest snowstorm.

I realized that just as I was looking at her world through the communicator, she was looking at mine through those photographs. The distance between Mars and our mountain was still forty-seven million whisker-lengths, but somehow it felt much smaller than it had three days before she left.

I held down the communicator button for a full ten seconds, which was my way of saying "Thank you for showing me that friendship stretches farther than I thought possible."

The next morning, I found a new message waiting: "Otto, I've discovered something remarkable. Mars soil, when mixed with earth minerals, can grow plants that are stronger than anything we've ever seen. When I come home, we should try some experiments in the cabin garden."

I spent the rest of that day planning what we'd plant first.

illustrations.description: A chubby baby otter with soft chestnut-brown fur, sparkling dark eyes, tiny paws, and floppy whiskers sits by the crackling fireplace in the cozy mountain cabin, holding the communication device which displays a colorful Martian sunrise on its screen. Snow falls outside the windows, and the warm firelight contrasts with the alien landscape shown on the device. Treasure map style illustration with aged parchment backgrounds, sepia tones, and jewel accents highlighting the glowing communicator screen.
illustrations.alt: Otto viewing his first picture from Mars by the fireplace

chapters.title: The Return Journey
chapters.content:
Two years and three months after Mabel left, I heard familiar engines rumbling up the mountain road.

I'd been expecting her return—the communicator had shown me pictures of her packing equipment and saying goodbye to the Mars research station—but hearing the actual sound of the transport vehicle made everything suddenly, wonderfully real.

I rushed outside, not bothering with a coat despite the spring snow that was still falling at this altitude.

The transport vehicle looked exactly the same as it had the day Mabel left, but when she climbed out, she looked different. Her hair was longer, and she moved with the careful balance of someone who'd spent a long time in lighter gravity. But her smile was exactly as I remembered.

"Otto!" she called. "You've grown!"

This was probably true, though I hadn't been paying careful attention to my size.

"How was Mars?" I asked as she gathered her equipment cases.

"Extraordinary. Cold. Dusty. Beautiful. Everything I hoped it would be and several things I didn't expect."

"What kinds of things you didn't expect?"

"Well," Mabel said, unlocking the cabin door, "I didn't expect to discover that Martian soil samples respond to music. When we played classical compositions in the greenhouse, the plants grew twenty percent faster."

"That's very surprising," I agreed.

Inside, the cabin welcomed us with familiar scents of pine and woodsmoke. I'd kept the fire going exactly as promised, and everything was just as Mabel had left it.

She unpacked her cases carefully, showing me each item as she removed it. Sealed containers of red Mars soil. Photographs of the research team. A small rock with crystals that caught the light like tiny stars.

"And this," she said, pulling out something I recognized, "is the Universal Adventure Assistant you built for me."

My invention looked well-used. The extending arms showed signs of frequent adjustment, and the bell had acquired a small dent that actually improved its sound.

"Did it work properly?" I asked.

"It worked perfectly. I used it almost every day. The mirror was especially helpful for examining soil samples, and the bell alerted me to several important discoveries."

"What kinds of discoveries?"

"Mineral deposits that might indicate ancient water sources. Rock formations that could provide shelter for future settlements. And once, a very small dust devil that was moving in an unusual pattern."

Mabel sat down in her favorite chair by the fire and looked around the cabin with the expression of someone remembering why they'd missed a place.

"Otto, I have a question for you."

"What kind of question?"

"The Space Research Division is planning a second Mars mission. This time, they're looking for researchers who want to establish permanent settlements. People who would help build the first real Martian communities."

I felt something cold settle in my stomach, even though I was sitting close to the fire.

"Are you going back?" I asked.

"Only if you want to come with me."

This was not what I'd expected her to say.

"Otters can go to Mars?"

"Inventive otters with excellent problem-solving skills and experience in friendship maintenance across long distances can go to Mars," Mabel corrected. "If they're interested in the adventure."

I looked around our cozy cabin, then at the Mars soil samples glittering in their containers, then at Mabel.

"Would there be a fireplace on Mars?"

"We'd build one."

"And would there be room for invention workshops?"

"All the room you could want."

"And would we be able to come back and visit the cabin?"

"Every few years, when the weather windows align properly."

I considered this carefully. Mars was still forty-seven million whisker-lengths away, but I'd learned that distance was less important than I'd originally calculated.

"When would we leave?" I asked.

"Next winter, when the snow gets deep and the cabin feels most like home. That way, we'll remember exactly what we're carrying with us."

Outside, the spring snow continued falling, but it looked different now. Instead of just weather, it looked like the beginning of preparation for the most extraordinary adventure either of us had ever planned.

"I think," I said finally, "that Mars could probably use some proper snow forts."

Mabel laughed. "I think you're absolutely right."

illustrations.description: A chubby baby otter with soft chestnut-brown fur, sparkling dark eyes, tiny paws, and floppy whiskers reunites with Mabel outside the snow-covered mountain cabin as she steps out of the transport vehicle. Her equipment cases are being unloaded, including the well-used Universal Adventure Assistant invention. Spring snow falls around them, and the cozy cabin glows warmly in the background. Treasure map style illustration with aged parchment feel, sepia tones, and jewel accents on the Mars equipment and communication devices.
illustrations.alt: Otto reuniting with Mabel as she returns from Mars

coverPrompt.description: A chubby baby otter with soft chestnut-brown fur, sparkling dark eyes, tiny paws, and floppy whiskers sits by a frosted window of a cozy mountain cabin. Outside, snow falls gently. On a table nearby, strange mechanical contraptions and star charts are scattered about. The illustration uses treasure map style with aged parchment backgrounds, sepia tones, and jewel accents highlighting the warm glow from the cabin's windows.
coverPrompt.alt: Otto the baby otter in a snowy mountain cabin with inventions scattered around

questions.question: What does Otto hear that makes him realize Mabel is packing?
choices: The attic floorboards creaking in a new pattern
questions.explanation: Otto notices the floorboards creaking in a pattern he'd never heard before, which leads him to discover Mabel packing for her Mars mission.

questions.question: What does Otto build to help Mabel with her journey?
questions.explanation: Otto creates a Universal Adventure Assistant with extending arms, a mirror, and a bell to help with exploration.

questions.question: How far does Otto calculate the distance to Mars?
questions.explanation: Otto uses his whiskers as measuring tools and determines Mars is forty-seven million whisker-lengths away.

questions.question: What does Mabel give Otto before she leaves?
questions.explanation: Mabel gives Otto a communicator that shows what she's looking at and can send firefly signals to her equipment.

questions.question: What surprising discovery does Mabel make about Mars plants?
questions.explanation: Mabel discovers that Martian plants grow 20% faster when classical music is played in the greenhouse.

questions.question: How long was Mabel's first mission to Mars?
questions.explanation: The story states that Mabel returns two years and three months after she left for Mars.

questions.question: What does Mabel ask Otto at the end of the story?
choices: To take care of the cabin forever
To come with her on the next mission
questions.explanation: Mabel invites Otto to join her on the second Mars mission to help establish permanent settlements.
